meta.descriptionTemplate: How we ship UN 1361 coconut charcoal from {{portWithCode}}: incoterms, export documents, transit times, dangerous-goods rules, and US import — MOQ {{moqLabel}}.

heroSubtitle: Logistics is the discipline of moving your container from our factory to your port. We load full 20ft containers {{portLabel}}, ship UN 1361 coconut charcoal as declared dangerous goods (Class {{unClass}}, Packing Group {{packingGroup}}), and hand your broker a complete export-document pack. Minimum order is {{moqLabel}}.

hero.ctaQuoteAria: Request a shipping quote on WhatsApp

atAGlance.heading: Shipping at a glance
atAGlance.definition: These are the load-bearing facts for planning an import: where the cargo loads, how it is classified, what documents travel with it, and how long it takes to reach your port.

intro.p1: This is the Logistics pillar for {{brand}}. It explains every stage between a confirmed order and a container on your dock: production, trucking to {{portFullName}}, dangerous-goods booking, the ocean voyage, and the import clearance your broker handles at destination.
intro.p2: Coconut shell charcoal is regulated cargo. Since IMDG Amendment 42-24 became mandatory on {{dgMandatoryDate}}, UN 1361 must be shipped as declared dangerous goods — there is no compliant non-DG route. Every page below treats that as the starting point, not a surprise at the port.
intro.p3: Customs clearance at destination is the buyer's responsibility. We load {{portLabel}}, issue the export documents, and the cargo crosses to your account at the ship's rail; your customs broker takes it from there.

rules.definition: The commercial rules of a charcoal order: how containers are loaded, which incoterms we offer, how payment is structured, and how a dangerous-goods sailing is booked.
facts: Full-container load (FCL) only — Class {{unClass}} cannot share an LCL groupage box.
Incoterms: {{incotermsList}}; our default is {{incotermDefault}} {{port}}.
Mixed sizes in one container are accommodated within a single grade order.

documents.definition: The paper that travels with your container. We issue {{documentsStandardCount}} standard documents with every shipment and {{documentsAdditionalCount}} more on request.
facts: Standard pack includes the commercial invoice, packing list, bill of lading, COO, MSDS, DGD and vanning certificate.
SHT and COA reports are available on request as paid add-ons.
Originals follow by courier; scans are sent first so your broker can pre-clear.

un1361.definition: UN 1361 "Carbon, animal or vegetable origin" is the classification under which coconut charcoal ships by sea — {{imdgLabel}}, Packing Group {{packingGroup}}.
facts: Class {{unClass}} means the cargo can self-heat; the SP 978 controls exist to prevent that.
The Self-Heating Test is supporting hazard evidence — no longer a route to non-DG status.
Self-heating fire is inherent vice and is not insurable — it is prevented, not insured.
un1361.linkLabel: What UN 1361 means for your shipment

dgRegulation.definition: What changed on 1 January {{dgMandatoryYear}}: IMDG Amendment 42-24 withdrew the old exemptions and introduced Special Provision 978, making declared dangerous goods the only lawful way to ship charcoal.
facts: Withdrew Special Provisions 925 and 223; the self-heating test no longer exempts.
SP 978 sets weathering, ≤ {{sp978PackTemp}} °C packing temperature, and {{sp978Headspace}} cm container headspace.
Any supplier quoting non-DG freight is offering a misdeclaration that exposes you to liability.

shippingLines.definition: Not every line carries Class {{unClass}} charcoal. We book directly with carriers that accept declared UN 1361 dangerous goods and hold the factory audits they require.
facts: Carriers we book directly: {{carriersAudited}}.
Several major lines have declined charcoal bookings — we name them so you can plan.
You may use your own forwarder under EXW or {{incotermDefault}}.

cargoProtection.definition: How the cargo is protected on the voyage, and what marine insurance does — and does not — cover. Insurance is arranged by the buyer under the incoterms we offer.
facts: Insurance covers external perils: water ingress, theft, general average, vessel casualty, external fire.
Self-heating fire is excluded (inherent vice) — prevention, not a policy, is the safeguard.
Moisture, thermal and breakage protection are explained; packaging holds the specs.

importUsa.definition: US import mechanics for HS {{hsCode6}} coconut charcoal: classification, the current duty stack, the entry process, and the FDA position on hookah charcoal.
facts: HTS candidates carry a Free base duty; a layered surcharge applies on top (date-stamped).
ISF 10+2, a customs bond, and CBP Form 7501 govern the entry.
Hookah charcoal is FDA-regulated as a tobacco-product component — read before you ship.

transit.heading: Transit times from {{port}}
transit.intro: Indicative ocean transit to the main buyer markets, ex-{{portWithCode}}. Day-ranges vary by line, routing, and season; confirm with the carrier at booking.
transit.sentenceTemplate: Typical ocean transit from {{portWithCode}} to {{destPort}} ({{unlocode}}) is {{min}}–{{max}} days.

timeline.heading: From order to your port
timeline.body: Total time has three parts. Production of a 20ft container runs {{leadTimeLabel}}. Once the cargo is ready it waits for the next dangerous-goods-capable sailing, then the ocean voyage to your port (see the transit table above). Booking is arranged during production, so it does not add to the front of the timeline.

dgOverview.heading: Dangerous goods, in one paragraph
dgOverview.body1: Coconut charcoal is {{imdgLabel}}, Packing Group {{packingGroup}}. Since {{dgMandatoryDate}} it must ship as declared dangerous goods under Special Provision 978 — there is no compliant non-DG route.
dgOverview.body2: Class {{unClass}} means the cargo can self-heat. The SP 978 controls — weathering, ≤ {{sp978PackTemp}} °C packing, {{sp978Headspace}} cm headspace — exist to prevent that, and incidents are rare. Self-heating fire is inherent vice and is not insurable, which is exactly why prevention, not a policy, is the safeguard.

items.q: What port do you ship from?
items.a: We load full containers {{portLabel}} — {{portWithCode}}. Surabaya (IDSUB) and Jakarta / Tanjung Priok (IDJKT) are available on request.

items.q: How long is transit to my port?
items.a: Indicative ocean transit ex-{{portWithCode}} ranges by destination — see the transit table above. Times vary by line and season; we confirm the current schedule with the carrier at booking.

items.q: How long from order to my port?
items.a: Production of a 20ft container runs {{leadTimeLabel}}. The cargo then waits for the next dangerous-goods-capable sailing before the ocean voyage to your port. Booking is arranged during production, so it does not add to the front of the timeline.

items.q: FCL only, or do you offer LCL?
items.a: Full-container load (FCL) only. UN 1361 is Class {{unClass}} dangerous goods and cannot be co-loaded with other cargo in an LCL groupage container — which is why the minimum order is {{moqLabel}}.
We offer {{incotermsList}}; our default basis is {{incotermDefault}} {{port}}. Under all three the buyer arranges marine insurance — we do not offer CIF.

Customs clearance at the destination is the buyer's responsibility, handled by your customs broker. We load {{portLabel}}, issue the export-document pack, and the cargo transfers to your account at the ship's rail.
Yes — a 40HQ carries {{net40ftTons}} net versus {{net20ftTons}} in a 20ft. Most buyers start with a 20ft container at the {{moqLabel}} minimum.
